🧾 1️⃣ What Is a User Story?

A user story is:

A small piece of business value the team can deliver in one sprint.

It is NOT just a requirement.

It answers:

Who is it for?

What do they need?

Why do they need it? (Business value)

👉 The “why” is what makes it powerful.

🏗 2️⃣ The Basic User Story Format

Use this structure:

As a [role]
I need [functionality]
So that [business value]

Example:

As a marketing manager
I need a list of customer emails
So that I can send promotions

Simple. Clear. Business-focused.

📌 3️⃣ Always Add Assumptions & Context

Add helpful details like:

System assumptions

Technical constraints

Dependencies

Business rules

This prevents confusion later.

👉 Give developers clarity without over-specifying.

✅ 4️⃣ Acceptance Criteria (Definition of Done) Is CRITICAL

This prevents arguments like:

“That’s not what I meant.”

Use Gherkin format:

Given (starting condition)

When (action happens)

Then (expected result)

Example:
Given 100 customers
And 90 opted in
When I request email list
Then I see 90 emails

👉 If it passes the criteria, it’s done.

No debates.

🔎 5️⃣ INVEST Rule (What Makes a Good Story)

Stories should be:

I – Independent
Can be prioritized separately.

N – Negotiable
Details can evolve.

V – Valuable
Delivers business value.

E – Estimable
Team can size it.

S – Small
Fits inside a sprint.

T – Testable
Can be verified.

👉 If it fails INVEST, rewrite it.

🧱 6️⃣ What Is an Epic?

An Epic is:

A large idea that is too big for one sprint.

Epics are:

Broken down into smaller stories.

Refined over time.

High-level containers for related stories.

👉 If you can’t finish it in one sprint → it’s probably an Epic.

🔄 7️⃣ How Work Flows

Big Idea → Epic → User Stories → Sprint → Done Increment

Epics shrink into Stories.
Stories get delivered.

🎯 What This Means for You as a PM

You must:

Focus on business value.

Write clear “why”.

Always define acceptance criteria.

Keep stories small.

Break down large ideas early.

Avoid vague stories.

Bad PMs write vague tasks.
Good PMs write clear value-driven stories.

Core Takeaway

A user story defines who needs what and why — with clear acceptance criteria — and small stories deliver value every sprint.

One-Line Summary

Great user stories are small, valuable, testable pieces of business value with clear definitions of done.
